---
title: "Course Blueprint"
---

## Overview

This page serves as the master design document for the course website. It gives the overall site map, instructional sequencing, learning goals, pacing, and the structural logic behind the repository.

## Primary Design Objectives

The course is built to satisfy six requirements simultaneously:

1. **Mathematical seriousness.** Core definitions, theorems, and derivations are stated carefully rather than gestured at.
2. **Computational modernity.** Every theoretical theme eventually connects to algorithms, tuning, diagnostics, or software workflow.
3. **Pedagogical repeatability.** Every chapter follows a consistent architecture so the course can be used for both self-study and formal instruction.
4. **Reproducibility.** Code is versioned, environments are documented, and deployment targets are explicit.
5. **Cross-language fluency.** `R`, `Stan`, and `JAX` each play a distinct role in the workflow.
6. **Direct expandability.** The outline pages are detailed enough that an instructor or author can extend them into full lecture notes, handouts, notebooks, or slides without redesigning the curriculum.

## Site Map

```text
Home
├── About This Course
│   ├── Teaching Philosophy
│   ├── Instructor & Credits
│   ├── Course Blueprint
│   ├── Schedule and Pacing
│   ├── Tools and Setup
│   ├── Evaluation
│   └── Chapter Expansion Map
├── Part I. Foundations
│   ├── Probability foundations
│   ├── Likelihood theory
│   └── Bayes' theorem and updating
├── Part II. Core Bayesian Inference
├── Part III. Computational Methods
│   ├── Monte Carlo methods
│   └── Markov chain Monte Carlo
├── Part IV. Bayesian Models
├── Part V. Optimization, Tuning, Diagnostics
├── Part VI. Software Workflows
│   ├── Sample chapter: Stan hierarchical workflow
│   └── Sample chapter: JAX gradient-based Bayes
├── Part VII. Advanced Topics
├── Exercises
├── Projects
├── Deployment and online execution
├── Appendices
│   ├── Notation
│   ├── Measure-probability review
│   └── Software setup
└── References
```

## Full Module Sequence

| Part | Chapters | Central question | Main outputs |
|---|---:|---|---|
| I. Foundations | 1-3 | What is probability, what is likelihood, and what does Bayesian updating mean? | Formal probability language, likelihood logic, posterior factorization |
| II. Core Bayesian Inference | 4-6 | How do priors, posteriors, prediction, and decisions interact? | Conjugate derivations, posterior risk, prior design workflow |
| III. Computation | 7-12 | How are posterior quantities approximated in practice? | Monte Carlo, MCMC, HMC, VI, SMC, online Bayes |
| IV. Models | 13-20 | Which model classes matter in modern Bayesian practice? | Regression, multilevel models, mixtures, time series, nonparametrics, spatial and causal models |
| V. Optimization and Diagnostics | 21-23 | How are algorithms tuned and models criticized? | MAP, calibration, SBC, PPCs, LOO, WAIC, divergences, sensitivity |
| VI. Software Workflows | 24-26 | How do we implement robust Bayesian workflows in actual tools? | Reproducible R, Stan, and JAX pipelines |
| VII. Advanced Topics | 27-31 | How do we handle model uncertainty, robustness, design, and communication? | BMA, stacking, robust Bayes, experimental design, capstones |

## Chapter Inventory

| Chapter | Title | Deliverable type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Probability foundations | Theory + derivations + simulation lab |
| 2 | Likelihood theory | Theory + asymptotics + comparison notes |
| 3 | Bayes' theorem and Bayesian paradigm | Theory + full sample chapter |
| 4 | Conjugate analysis | Derivations + posterior predictive lab |
| 5 | Loss functions and decision theory | Theory + worked decisions |
| 6 | Prior design | Workflow + sensitivity lab |
| 7 | Numerical integration and approximation | Approximation methods + comparison lab |
| 8 | Monte Carlo methods | Simulation + ESS calculations |
| 9 | Markov chain Monte Carlo | Theory + full sample chapter |
| 10 | Advanced MCMC | HMC/NUTS geometry + diagnostics |
| 11 | Variational inference | ELBO derivation + ADVI notes |
| 12 | Sequential and online computation | Particle methods + state-space workflow |
| 13 | Bayesian linear models | Derivations + shrinkage examples |
| 14 | Generalized linear models | Logistic/Poisson/ordinal workflow |
| 15 | Hierarchical and multilevel models | Partial pooling + funnel geometry |
| 16 | Mixture and latent variable models | Identifiability + label switching |
| 17 | Time series and state-space models | Kalman filtering + smoothing |
| 18 | Nonparametric Bayesian methods | DP and GP overview with derivations |
| 19 | Spatial and graphical models | Conditional independence + structured priors |
| 20 | Bayesian causal inference | Potential outcomes + sensitivity |
| 21 | Optimization techniques | MAP and gradient methods |
| 22 | Tuning and calibration | Hyperparameters + algorithm control |
| 23 | Diagnostics and model criticism | PPC, SBC, LOO, WAIC, divergences |
| 24 | Bayesian workflow in R | Reproducible analysis toolkit |
| 25 | Bayesian workflow in Stan | Full sample chapter |
| 26 | Bayesian workflow in JAX | Full sample chapter |
| 27 | Model selection and averaging | Bayes factors, stacking, projection |
| 28 | Robust Bayesian methods | Heavy tails, contamination, misspecification |
| 29 | Bayesian experimental design | Utility-based design |
| 30 | Communication and interpretation | Reporting and decision summaries |
| 31 | Capstone projects | Full project pipeline |

## Learning Goals By Part

### Part I. Foundations

By the end of Part I, learners should be able to:

- state probability models using measurable spaces and conditional distributions;
- distinguish probability statements from likelihood statements;
- derive posterior distributions from Bayes' theorem in both discrete and density-based settings;
- explain exchangeability and sequential updating as core Bayesian ideas.

### Part II. Core Bayesian Inference

Learners should be able to:

- derive conjugate posteriors, predictive distributions, and marginal likelihoods;
- compute Bayes estimators under several loss functions;
- design informative, weakly informative, and reference-style priors;
- perform prior predictive checks and sensitivity analysis.

### Part III. Computation

Learners should be able to:

- approximate posterior expectations using quadrature, Laplace approximation, and importance sampling;
- implement and tune Metropolis-Hastings and Gibbs samplers;
- explain the geometry and adaptation logic of HMC and NUTS;
- compare MCMC, variational inference, and sequential methods in terms of fidelity, scalability, and failure modes.

### Part IV. Models

Learners should be able to:

- build Bayesian versions of classical regression and generalized linear models;
- interpret shrinkage and partial pooling in multilevel models;
- reason about latent allocations, label switching, and state-space structure;
- connect nonparametric, spatial, and causal models to their conditional independence assumptions.

### Part V. Optimization, Tuning, Diagnostics

Learners should be able to:

- connect MAP estimation to penalized optimization;
- tune MCMC, HMC, and variational algorithms with principled diagnostics;
- use posterior predictive checks, simulation-based calibration, and cross-validation to criticize models;
- report weaknesses and sensitivity rather than only point estimates.

### Part VI-VII. Software and Advanced Topics

Learners should be able to:

- run reproducible Bayesian workflows in `R`, `Stan`, and `JAX`;
- choose among model comparison tools such as LOO, stacking, and Bayes factors;
- formulate robust Bayesian models for misspecified or outlier-prone data;
- communicate posterior uncertainty in a decision-oriented and reproducible way.

## Recommended Pacing

### Semester Format: 14 Weeks

| Weeks | Coverage | Main work |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | Chapters 1-3 | Foundations and Bayes theorem |
| 3-4 | Chapters 4-6 | Conjugacy, decision theory, priors |
| 5-7 | Chapters 7-10 | Numerical methods, Monte Carlo, MCMC, HMC |
| 8 | Chapter 11-12 | VI and sequential methods |
| 9-11 | Chapters 13-20 | Modeling block |
| 12 | Chapters 21-23 | Optimization, tuning, diagnostics |
| 13 | Chapters 24-26 | Software workflows |
| 14 | Chapters 27-31 | Advanced topics and project presentations |

### Self-Study Format: 28 Weeks

- Use one week per chapter for Chapters 1-20.
- Use two weeks for Chapters 9, 10, 15, 23, 25, and 26 because these are computationally dense.
- Reserve two additional weeks for project work and review.

### Graduate Reading Course

For a mathematically mature student working with an advisor:

- treat Parts I-II as a compressed review unless probability foundations are weak;
- spend most effort on Parts III-VI;
- require one project that includes prior sensitivity, diagnostics, and predictive evaluation.

## Assessment Design

Recommended assessment mix:

- weekly or biweekly derivation sets;
- computational labs in `R` and `Stan`;
- one short algorithmic implementation assignment in `JAX`;
- one model criticism memo based on PPC, LOO, and sensitivity analysis;
- one capstone project with a written report and reproducible code.

## Repository And Folder Structure

The course is organized to make authoring and future expansion straightforward:

```text
.
├── _quarto.yml
├── index.qmd
├── teaching-philosophy.qmd
├── instructor-and-credits.qmd
├── course-blueprint.qmd
├── syllabus.qmd
├── evaluation.qmd
├── chapter-outline.qmd
├── parts/
├── chapters/
│   ├── foundations/
│   ├── computation/
│   └── software/
├── appendix/
├── bibliography/
├── labs/
│   ├── r/
│   ├── stan/
│   └── jax/
├── styles/
└── .github/workflows/
```

## Citation Strategy

The course uses three citation layers:

1. **Canonical foundations** such as @bernardo1994, @berger1985, @jeffreys1961, and @gelman2013.
2. **Landmark methodological papers** such as @metropolis1953, @hastings1970, @hoffman2014, @kucukelbir2017, and @vehtari2017.
3. **Software references** such as @carpenter2017, @cmdstanr2024, @posterior2024, and @jax2018.

This allows individual chapters to combine theory, algorithms, and software documentation without breaking citation consistency.
